display_name: NTP stratum 16 means the clock is unsynchronized, not that stratum 16 is a valid source
context: show ntp status or show ntp associations showing stratum 16
observation: NTP source or local clock shows stratum 16
naive_interpretation: The device is synchronized to a stratum 16 NTP source — just a very distant one in the hierarchy
correct_interpretation: Stratum 16 is a special value defined in RFC 5905 meaning unsynchronized. Valid NTP strata range from 1 (reference clock) to 15. Stratum 16 means the device has no valid time source — it is free-running on its internal clock. Any other device peering with this one will also go unsynchronized.
why: RFC 5905 Section 7.1 defines stratum 16 as MAXSTRAT — a kiss-of-death value meaning the clock is not synchronized
Valid strata are 1-15; stratum 0 is reserved for reference clocks (GPS, atomic)
A device showing stratum 16 will cause downstream peers to also lose sync — this cascades

confirmation_commands.purpose: Check if the clock is actually synchronized and what stratum it claims
Check the status of all configured NTP peers — look for a selected peer (marked with *)
Check peer reachability and status — a peer with reach 0 has never responded

traps: Assuming stratum 16 is just a high-numbered but valid stratum
Not realizing that an unsynchronized device will poison the time of downstream NTP clients
Ignoring NTP unsync issues because the network seems to work — until certificate validation or logging timestamps break
